stories.body: Reuters reports that Iran has yet to answer a proposed interim agreement with the United States to halt their war, insisting on stern review because of mistrust over past U.S. compliance, even as President Donald Trump predicts a ceasefire extension that would reopen the Strait of Hormuz; the conflict, which has largely closed the vital waterway and sparked parallel fighting in Lebanon, has killed thousands and left oil markets on edge while Tehran seeks sanctions relief, access to frozen revenues and leverage over maritime chokepoints before agreeing to any limited deal.
stories.headline: Iran ties reopening of the Strait of Hormuz to a still-uncertain war-ending deal

stories.body: According to Reuters, U.S. Central Command said American forces downed four Iranian drones aimed at vessels near the Strait of Hormuz and then hit surveillance radars at Goruk and Qeshm Island, underscoring how tit-for-tat strikes are complicating negotiations for an interim truce as Tehran demands sanctions relief, port access and continued leverage over the nearly blocked strait while Trump faces rising domestic anger over gas prices and a war he vowed would end quickly.
stories.headline: U.S. strikes Iranian coastal radar sites after drones targeted Hormuz shipping

stories.body: Reuters notes that NASA briefly ordered five astronauts into their Crew Dragon safe-haven when air losses in Russia’s Zvezda module doubled to about two pounds per day; the evacuation was rescinded after two hours once Roscosmos paused its plan to saw through paneling to reach one of two leaks, highlighting months of disagreement over cause and repair methods even as the seven-person crew, including NASA’s Crew-12 team, continues work on the 27-year-old station that Congress is weighing extending to 2032.
stories.headline: NASA orders ISS crew into safe-haven as worst air leak yet tests U.S.-Russia cooperation

stories.body: Reuters reports that Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi told Lebanon’s Al Mayadeen TV the Iran-U.S. war cannot end without an Israeli pullout from southern Lebanon, reiterating Tehran’s backing of Hezbollah just as the group rejected a U.S.-brokered ceasefire that lacked withdrawal terms; despite partial truces elsewhere, Israel keeps striking southern Lebanese towns, Iran warns it will respond to attacks on Beirut, and Trump says Lebanon deserves peace even as Iran links any Strait of Hormuz reopening to a regional settlement that protects its allies.
stories.headline: Tehran doubles down on Hezbollah and demands Israeli withdrawal as ceasefire diplomacy falters

stories.body: Reuters eyewitnesses saw heavy Russian attacks on Kyiv that set vehicles ablaze in the Obolon district and sparked fires at a Podil non-residential site and a nine-story apartment building, prompting officials to urge residents into shelters while President Volodymyr Zelenskiy warned a massive strike remains possible after Moscow threatened systematic hits on command centers in retaliation for a deadly drone strike in Russian-occupied Luhansk, which Ukraine denies carrying out.
stories.headline: Russian barrage ignites fires across Kyiv amid warnings of renewed massive strikes

stories.body: BBC News and The Guardian describe a Swiss People’s Party “No to a Switzerland with 10 million” initiative that would force the government to impose strict limits on asylum, family reunification and free movement once the population hits 9.5 million, potentially requiring withdrawal from the EU single market if the 10 million cap is breached before 2050; the government, major parties, unions and business groups call it a chaos plan that would starve hospitals and hotels of workers, yet polls show a tight race as voters fret over housing, transit congestion and rising health costs in a country where 27 percent of residents are foreign and population growth has surged since 2002.
stories.headline: Switzerland heads to a referendum on capping population at 10 million

stories.body: Politico reports that Senate Republicans are invoking the U.S.-hosted World Cup’s massive security demands to pressure Democrats into extending Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act, which is expiring amid congressional turmoil over Trump’s controversial choice of Bill Pulte as acting director of national intelligence; GOP lawmakers warn a terror attack would trigger finger-pointing, while Democrats argue Trump’s personnel moves derailed a near-complete long-term deal even as millions of foreign visitors arrive and gas prices stay elevated by the Iran war.
stories.headline: World Cup security fears fuel new GOP attacks on Democrats over lapsed FISA powers

stories.body: According to Politico, Republican strategists, party chairs and candidates now view Labor Day as the deadline for President Donald Trump to wrap up the damaging war with Iran, warning that persistent casualties, five-dollar gas and fertilizer shortages could doom their midterm hopes; while a White House official pegs the odds of a preliminary truce at up to 85 percent, Republicans openly fret about repeated missed timelines, rising anti-war sentiment within their base, and vulnerable incumbents who already broke with Trump on war powers to show impatience with the grinding conflict.
stories.headline: Trump’s GOP allies draw a Labor Day line for ending the Iran war before the midterms

stories.body: Politico details how Freedom 250, a nonprofit aligned with Trump, is staging a June 25–July 10 “Great American State Fair” on the National Mall that features conservative groups such as America Prays, the American Principles Project, Hillsdale College and Focus on the Family, drawing accusations from Democrats that the president is politicizing the nation’s 250th anniversary and prompting congressional probes into fundraising; despite several states’ refusal to participate and musical acts dropping out over Trump ties, organizers promise a broad celebration alongside a June 24 Trump rally after he dismissed the need for high-priced performers.
stories.headline: Freedom 250’s National Mall fair spotlights conservative groups amid scrutiny over the semiquincentennial

stories.body: Politico reports that Rep. Nancy Mace was eliminated in the South Carolina GOP gubernatorial primary after scandals, millions in attack ads and Donald Trump’s late endorsement of Lt. Gov. Pamela Evette eroded her support, marking another defeat for Republicans who crossed the president on issues such as releasing Jeffrey Epstein files; Evette now faces Attorney General Alan Wilson in a June 23 runoff, while Mace, who forfeited her House seat to run for governor, hints she could spend her remaining months in Congress disrupting Trump’s agenda after allies like Reps. Thomas Massie and Sen. Bill Cassidy were also toppled for defying him.
stories.headline: Nancy Mace’s primary loss in South Carolina governor race underscores Trump’s sway

stories.body: El País, El Mundo and RTVE say National Court judge José Luis Calama has carved out a separate case from the Plus Ultra investigation to examine potential tax fraud and contraband linked to more than a hundred luxury jewels worth €1.323 million found in former prime minister José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero’s office, arguing that the lack of traceable acquisition records could imply unpaid VAT, transfer, inheritance or income taxes above the €120,000 criminal threshold and summoning him to answer questions on June 17–18 alongside the main influence-peddling probe over the airline rescue.
stories.headline: Judge opens new probe into Zapatero over €1.3 million in seized jewels

stories.body: El País recounts that Pope León XIV concluded his Canaries visit with a second day of meetings and embraces with migrants at Tenerife’s Las Raíces center, stressing that “the foreigner of yesterday can be the brother and neighbor of today” on the same day the EU’s Migration and Asylum Pact took effect with stricter controls and faster expulsions, a juxtaposition that challenged right-wing narratives during a tour devoted to denouncing polarization and hostility toward immigrants.
stories.headline: Pope León XIV ends Spain trip embracing migrants as EU enforces tougher border rules

stories.body: El País and El Mundo report that the Court of Justice of the European Union will rule on July 16 on whether Spain’s Catalan amnesty law endangers EU financial interests or conflicts with anti-terror rules, addressing questions from the Court of Auditors about the fiscal liability of former Catalan presidents Carles Puigdemont and Artur Mas and from the National Court about terrorism charges against CDR activists, a decision expected to guide Spain’s Constitutional Court as it weighs Puigdemont’s appeal after an earlier opinion by Advocate General Dean Spielmann backed the amnesty’s legality.
stories.headline: EU court sets July 16 ruling on Spain’s amnesty law questions

stories.body: El País and El Mundo note that Spain’s Popular Party has summoned Prosecutor General Teresa Peramato to a June 19 Senate Justice Committee hearing to explain unregistered meetings between her aide Diego Villafañe, PSOE fixer Leire Díez and lawyer Jacobo Teijelo, while also demanding Interior Minister Fernando Grande-Marlaska return to answer for the “case Koldo” and scheduling testimonies from figures linked to the Plus Ultra rescue in what the opposition calls an effort to expose “state delinquency” tied to the Sánchez government.
stories.headline: PP hauls Spain’s prosecutor general and interior minister to the Senate over Leire Díez meetings

stories.body: Tot Barcelona reports that Spain’s National Police removed roughly 600 singers who were set to accompany the Escolania de Montserrat during Pope Lleó XIV’s blessing of the Sagrada Família’s Jesus Tower after officers allegedly found estelades hidden in sheet music and suspected a plan to display the Catalan independence flag and sing “Els Segadors,” forcing organizers to use a recording while Mossos d’Esquadra kettled the expelled choir outside for about 15 minutes.
stories.headline: Police eject pro-independence choir before papal mass at the Sagrada Família

stories.body: 3Cat Barcelona says TramBesòs lines T4, T5 and T6 will be partially closed July 3–September 13 for Glòries-area track realignment that includes double-tracking between Badajoz and Bilbao streets, with replacement buses, traffic detours on Avinguda Diagonal, bike lane closures and continued work into autumn that maintains restrictions between Glòries and Sant Martí de Provençals, while TMB will simultaneously renew L1 track between Santa Eulàlia and Mercat Nou, upgrade the Catalunya L1–L3 transfer, close L4’s Verdaguer station from July 6 to August 30, and shut L9/L10 Nord between La Sagrera and Bon Pastor in two phases with bus bridges.
stories.headline: Barcelona braces for summer shutdowns on tram lines T4–T6 and key metro segments

stories.body: Tot Barcelona notes that Catalonia’s agriculture department has told 19 municipalities the restrictions on accessing Collserola and other zones hit by African swine fever outbreaks will remain through at least late summer to keep the virus from reaching pig farms, despite months of protests from residents and proposals such as Sant Cugat del Vallès’s idea for tightly controlled pilot reopenings in isolated areas like Volpelleres or Torre Negra after seven months of closures and mounting civil disobedience walks.
stories.headline: Collserola park to stay closed at least until late summer because of wildfire danger
